content.text: Symptom-Based Alert Design for Claude Services

Most alert fatigue stems from alerting on causes — internal state changes like memory usage, queue depth, or retry counts — rather than on symptoms: the degraded experience your users actually observe. Symptom-based alerting flips this: you only page on-call when something a user would notice has changed. For Claude integrations, the observable symptoms are slow responses, high error rates, degraded output quality, and budget exhaustion.
This document explains the symptom-first design principle, maps it to specific alert rules for Claude services, and shows how to write YAML rules that combine with your existing Prometheus setup.

) fires when internal state changes. The internal state may or may not affect users. Because the alert fires without user impact, on-call engineers learn to ignore it — and then miss the one time it coincides with a real problem.
A symptom-based alert fires when something that

degrades. The question to ask when writing any alert is: "if this fires at 3am, would a user have already noticed?" If the answer is no, it is a cause-based alert — convert it to a metric that users observe, or move it to a diagnostic (non-paging) channel.
Error rate > SLO budget burn rate
Token budget < N days remaining at current rate
Quality eval score below accepted threshold
Cause-based metrics are still valuable — as diagnostic signals on dashboards, visible when an on-call engineer investigates a symptom alert. They should not page by themselves.
The Four Symptom Categories for Claude Services
Claude integration symptoms fall into four categories, each corresponding to a distinct user impact:
Responses feel slow or time out

Each category has a warning level (engineer investigates next business day) and a page level (on-call wakes up now). The gap between them should be wide enough that warnings are not silently ignored — if warnings routinely don't lead to action, the threshold is wrong.

groups:
  - name: claude_latency
    rules:
      - alert: ClaudeLatencyWarning
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum by (feature, le)(
              rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])
            )
          ) > 5
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude p99 latency elevated for {{ $labels.feature }}"
          description: >
            p99 latency is {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}.
            SLO threshold is 5s. Check for rate limiting or model overload.
          runbook: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/claude-latency

      - alert: ClaudeLatencyPage
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum by (feature, le)(
              rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])
            )
          ) > 30
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude p99 latency critical for {{ $labels.feature }}"
          description: >
            p99 latency is {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}.
            Users are experiencing severe timeout risk.
          runbook: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/claude-latency

      - alert: ClaudeTTFTHigh
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            sum by (feature, le)(
              rate(claude_time_to_first_token_seconds_bucket[5m])
            )
          ) > 10
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude TTFT p95 high for {{ $labels.feature }}"
          description: "Streaming first token is slow ({{ $value | humanizeDuration }})."

- alert: ClaudeErrorRateWarning
        expr: |
          sum by (feature)(rate(claude_api_error_total[5m]))
          /
          sum by (feature)(rate(claude_api_request_total[5m]))
          > 0.05
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude error rate > 5% for {{ $labels.feature }}"
          description: >
            Error rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            Common causes: rate limiting (429), model overload (529), or
            application-side timeout (408).
          runbook: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/claude-errors

      - alert: ClaudeErrorRatePage
        expr: |
          sum by (feature)(rate(claude_api_error_total[5m]))
          /
          sum by (feature)(rate(claude_api_request_total[5m]))
          > 0.20
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude error rate critical for {{ $labels.feature }}"

      - alert: ClaudeRateLimitSustained
        expr: |
          sum by (feature)(
            rate(claude_api_error_total{error_category="rate_limit"}[15m])
          ) > 0.5
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Sustained rate limiting on Claude for {{ $labels.feature }}"
          description: >
            Rate limit errors persisting > 10 minutes. Consider adding
            per-user rate limiting, request coalescing, or contacting Anthropic
            for quota increase.

- alert: ClaudeTokenBudgetLow
        expr: claude_token_budget_remaining_pct < 0.20
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude token budget below 20% for {{ $labels.feature }}"
          description: >
            Token budget is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} remaining.
            Current burn rate will exhaust budget in approximately
            {{ with query "claude_token_budget_hours_remaining" }}{{ . | first | value | humanizeDuration }}{{ end }}.

      - alert: ClaudeTokenBudgetCritical
        expr: claude_token_budget_remaining_pct < 0.05
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude token budget critical for {{ $labels.feature }}"
          description: >
            Token budget is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} remaining.
            Feature may become unavailable imminently.
An alert is only as useful as the context it delivers to the on-call engineer. Every alert must include these annotations:
p99 is 42s. SLO is 30s. Check for rate limiting or model overload.
URL to the relevant Grafana dashboard panel
warning | page (as a label, not annotation)
in descriptions so engineers see the actual number without opening a dashboard. Use Prometheus template functions:

The runbook URL in the alert annotation is the single most actionable thing you can add. Without it, engineers spend the first five minutes of an incident searching for the playbook. Every alert should link to a runbook before it is merged.

Standard threshold alerts have a fatal flaw: a slow, sustained error rate that stays just below the threshold never pages — but it still burns through your monthly error budget. Multi-burn-rate alerts solve this by asking: "how fast is the error budget being consumed?"

# SLO target: 99.5% success rate (0.5% error budget over 30 days)
# This means we can tolerate 0.005 * 30 * 24 * 3600 = 12960 error-seconds per month.

groups:
  - name: claude_slo_burn
    rules:
      # Fast burn: 14x — burns the full monthly budget in ~2 days → PAGE
      - alert: ClaudeSLOBurnRateFast
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_error_total[1h])) / sum(rate(claude_api_request_total[1h]))
            > (14 * 0.005)
          )
          and
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_error_total[5m])) / sum(rate(claude_api_request_total[5m]))
            > (14 * 0.005)
          )
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude SLO fast burn: budget exhausted in ~2 days"
          description: >
            Current error rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            At this rate the 30-day error budget will be exhausted in ~2 days.

      # Slow burn: 6x — burns the full budget in ~5 days → WARNING
      - alert: ClaudeSLOBurnRateSlow
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_error_total[6h])) / sum(rate(claude_api_request_total[6h]))
            > (6 * 0.005)
          )
          and
          (
            sum(rate(claude_api_error_total[30m])) / sum(rate(claude_api_request_total[30m]))
            > (6 * 0.005)
          )
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude SLO slow burn: budget exhausted in ~5 days"
The dual-window condition (1h AND 5m for fast; 6h AND 30m for slow) ensures the alert fires only when the burn rate is both current
sustained — not just a transient spike or a stale slow-window average.
When the Claude API itself is down (a service-level outage), all feature-specific latency and error alerts fire simultaneously. Inhibition rules suppress child alerts when a parent alert fires:

# alertmanager.yml — inhibit_rules section
inhibit_rules:
  # If the global Claude API outage alert is firing, suppress per-feature alerts
  - source_matchers:
      - alertname = ClaudeAPIOutage
    target_matchers:
      - alertname =~ "ClaudeLatency.*|ClaudeError.*|ClaudeRateLimit.*"
    equal:
      - env         # only inhibit in the same environment

  # Critical alert suppresses its own warning
  - source_matchers:
      - severity = page
    target_matchers:
      - severity = warning
    equal:
      - alertname
      - feature
as an alert that fires when all features simultaneously show elevated error rates — a signature of an upstream API problem rather than a feature-specific bug:

- alert: ClaudeAPIOutage
        expr: |
          count(
            sum by (feature)(rate(claude_api_error_total[5m]))
            /
            sum by (feature)(rate(claude_api_request_total[5m]))
            > 0.50
          ) >= 3
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Claude API outage: 3+ features showing > 50% error rate"
          description: >
            {{ $value }} features are experiencing >50% error rates simultaneously.
            This is likely an upstream Anthropic API issue, not a local problem.
            Check https://status.anthropic.com
to unit-test alert definitions before deploying them:

# tests/alert_test.yaml
rule_files:
  - ../alerts/claude_latency.yaml
  - ../alerts/claude_errors.yaml

tests:
  - interval: 1m
    input_series:
      # Simulate p99 latency crossing 5s threshold
      - series: 'claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{feature="support-bot",le="5"}'
        values: '0+100x10'
      - series: 'claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{feature="support-bot",le="+Inf"}'
        values: '0+200x10'  # 50% of requests exceed 5s → p99 well above 5s

    alert_rule_test:
      - eval_time: 10m
        alertname: ClaudeLatencyWarning
        exp_alerts:
          - exp_labels:
              severity: warning
              feature: support-bot
            exp_annotations:
              summary: "Claude p99 latency elevated for support-bot"

# Run alert tests in CI
promtool test rules tests/alert_test.yaml

Symptom-based alerts for Claude services page on user-visible outcomes — latency, error rate, token budget exhaustion, quality degradation — rather than internal state that may or may not affect users. The result is lower alert fatigue, faster incident response, and higher trust in the paging system.
Ask "would a user already notice?" before adding any page-level alert.
Map every alert to one of four symptom categories: latency, errors, token budget, or quality.
Include summary, description, runbook, and dashboard URL annotations on every alert.
Use multi-window multi-burn-rate expressions to catch slow, sustained budget drain that threshold alerts miss.
Add inhibition rules to suppress per-feature alerts when an upstream Claude API outage fires.
Test alert rules with `promtool test rules` before deploying to production.
